title: Nonlinear Electro-Optic Visible Photonic Circuits for Solid-State Quantum Defects
summary_bullets: This work demonstrates a monolithic thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) photonic circuit that integrates a periodically poled frequency-doubler (PPLN) with a GHz-bandwidth electro‑optic Mach–Zehnder modulator to produce and gate visible (532 nm) light on‑chip, delivering off‑chip green power exceeding 1 mW with an extinction ratio above 42 dB. The device exploits the quadratic dependence of second‑harmonic generation to square the pump modulation and achieve a nonlinear contrast enhancement that overcomes the usual EO‑modulator extinction–bandwidth tradeoff without active feedback. As a proof‑of‑concept the authors demonstrate nanosecond gating and coherent spin control of single NV centers (pulsed ODMR, Rabi oscillations, Ramsey) and time‑resolved lifetime measurements using time‑tagged photon counting.

Yanbei would care because the platform enables high‑speed, high‑contrast optical control and on‑chip visible light generation for solid‑state spin systems—capabilities that directly affect quantum‑limited readout, fast temporal gating of measurements, and the scaling of light–matter interfaces he studies; the nonlinear contrast enhancement in particular offers a way to suppress background photons and leakage without extra feedback, which is valuable for low‑noise quantum measurements and for experiments that require precise, time‑resolved control (e.g., entanglement distribution or tests of gravity‑induced decoherence/entanglement using spin systems).

Keywords/topics: integrated visible photonics, thin‑film lithium niobate (TFLN), periodically poled lithium niobate (PPLN), second‑harmonic generation (SHG), electro‑optic (EO) modulation, NV center coherent control, GHz gating, high extinction ratio, scalable quantum nodes; very recent — arXiv:2603.21751 (physics.optics), posted 23 Mar 2026.
